Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BlackRock identifies US value stocks as offering a historic re-rating opportunity, trading at a 43% discount to the broad market versus a 19% historical median on data back to 1978. The firm's systematic research shows the value factor delivers its strongest risk-adjusted returns during periods of accelerating GDP growth — precisely the regime the team expects. With earnings broadening beyond mega-caps and markets placing greater weight on cost discipline and competitive durability, the balance favours value over growth. The MyMap multi-asset range acted on this view in April, introducing a new US Value exposure as an undervalued area of the market well positioned for the ongoing expansion in real economic activity, particularly in choppy market conditions. MyMap introduced a new US Value position, expecting returns to broaden beyond the handful of companies that have driven markets. Value provides access to relatively undervalued areas of the market and is well positioned to benefit from the expansion in real economic activity. It has historically performed well in choppy market conditions. The Equity Market Outlook provides the deeper factor case: US Value is trading at a 43% discount to the market vs a 19% historical median (back to 1978), implying material re-rating opportunity simply to revert to long-term norms.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: European defence stocks have gained approximately 350% over three years driven by the Ukraine war and NATO spending commitments, yet BlackRock still sees value. When adjusting for expected earnings growth, European defence valuations on a PEG basis appear far cheaper than US peers, which sit at long-term highs. NATO-driven structural spending increases provide a multi-year tailwind.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: UK small caps offer unique value relative to their 20-year history, with only Mexican stocks showing a bigger discount on the same basis. BlackRock believes potential Bank of England rate cuts could catalyse a re-rating of these domestically focused stocks, though higher-for-longer inflation poses a risk to the thesis.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BlackRock identifies healthcare as one of the most mature sectors for AI deployment, with meaningful early adoption in imaging, diagnostics, and documentation among hospitals, insurers, and medical technology companies. Drug discovery is an emerging frontier. However, the opportunity remains early-stage overall and the team frames it as a watch-and-build rather than a high-conviction overweight.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII is neutral on Chinese equities. Trade relations with the US have steadied but property stress and an aging population still constrain the macro outlook. Relatively resilient activity limits near-term policy urgency. BII likes sectors like AI, automation and power generation within China.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII is neutral on short US Treasuries, noting that shorter-term bonds are relatively attractive as the market has woken up to persistent inflation and higher rates. The front end offers better risk-adjusted carry than the long end.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII is neutral on Chinese government bonds. China bonds offer stability and diversification, but developed market yields are now higher and investor sentiment is shifting towards equities, limiting upside for bonds.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII is underweight long-term IG credit, preferring short-term bonds that are less exposed to interest rate risk. With rising term premia and energy-driven inflation pressures, the duration exposure in long-term IG is unattractive.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: MyMap positions high yield bonds in the positive segment of their views dial, reflecting the attractive income and shorter duration characteristics of the asset class in the current environment.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: MyMap opened a new position in inflation-linked bonds to protect portfolios against rising inflation from the energy supply shock. The team identifies the energy price surge from the Middle East conflict as elevating the likelihood of an inflation uptick.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: MyMap positions Pacific ex-Japan equities in the negative segment of their views dial. The region's heavy dependence on energy imports via the Strait of Hormuz makes it particularly exposed to the ongoing supply shock.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: MyMap shows government bonds in the negative segment of their views dial. The team's 'mind the debt iceberg' theme highlights concern about deteriorating public finances, rising defence budgets, and elevated interest rates. They prefer shorter-dated bonds and are becoming increasingly selective about lending duration.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII upgrades EM equities to overweight in the Global Outlook, staying selective with a preference for Asian countries manufacturing critical AI components (South Korea, Taiwan) and Latin American energy and commodity exporters. This is an upgrade from neutral in the Weekly Commentary. The MyMap multi-asset range also retains its existing EM tilt for the same reasons — cheap valuations and robust profits — adding multi-paper reinforcement to the OW stance.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII downgrades Euro area government bonds from overweight to neutral in the Global Outlook. The market has repriced the ECB policy path more in line with BII's view, and increased German bond issuance to finance fiscal stimulus is already largely reflected in current 10-year yields.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026
Full Commentary: BII upgrades infrastructure equity to overweight in the 2026 Global Outlook Q2 Update, underpinned by mega forces including AI buildout, energy transition, and defence spending. Listed infrastructure trades near 2-standard-deviation discounts to global equity — among the cheapest thematic exposures available — and offers structural demand resilience. BlackRock has also acted on this view at the multi-asset level: the MyMap range added a new holding in listed infrastructure during the Q1 update (April), framing it as an undervalued real-economy asset well positioned to benefit from the ongoing expansion in real economic activity, complementing MyMap's value tilt.

Source: Equity Market Outlook Q2 2026 | MyMap Quarterly Update - Q1 2026
Full Commentary: BII still likes private credit strategically but sees dispersion ahead, stressing the importance of manager selection. Private credit is entering a normalized but increasingly uneven phase, with stress concentrated in areas with less margin for error. Direct lending is the preferred sub-sector.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: GSAM identifies critical minerals as the single most important physical input underpinning every major secular theme — AI, defense, clean energy, and electrification. China refines 19 of 20 strategic minerals, creating severe supply-chain concentration risk. Lithium demand could rise fivefold by 2040; graphite and nickel demand is expected to double. The team argues the strategic premium sits at 'structural chokepoints' — companies and sectors that control, process, or recycle these inputs.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Gold is positioned as the ultimate hedge against geopolitical tail risk and currency debasement. GSAM's CIO Digest introduces the 'HALO' trade thesis — Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence — suggesting gold's rally represents a structural regime shift as markets rotate out of asset-light technology sectors into asset-heavy sectors. In a global-recession scenario, gold serves as the primary portfolio hedge.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Copper faces a projected 30% supply deficit by 2035 as AI data-centre, defense, and electrification demand converge. Every AI cluster requires grid buildout, and every grid buildout requires copper. GSAM highlights copper alongside gold as essential commodity positioning within the HALO trade framework, reflecting sustained demand from the physical infrastructure layer of the digital economy.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Clean energy equities have rebounded sharply, with the Bloomberg GS Clean Energy Index gaining 33% in 2025 versus 14.4% for MSCI World Energy. Global energy-transition spending hit $2.3tn in 2025, over 5x global data-centre capex. GSAM sees a multi-bucket opportunity spanning miners, industrial capex beneficiaries, utilities with contracted renewable revenues, and semiconductor equipment suppliers benefiting from a sustained capex cycle.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Green bonds are positioned as the most scalable fixed-income instrument to capture Europe's energy-transition financing requirement of ~€660bn per year to 2030. The EU Green Bond Standard reduces greenwashing risk through taxonomy alignment, mandatory disclosure, and external verification. GSAM research shows diversification benefits with minimal impact on duration or yield, making green bonds a structural allocation rather than a satellite sleeve.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: GSAM favours US large caps for their relative insulation from the Middle East conflict and continued AI momentum. Strong corporate fundamentals evidenced by recent earnings season support the view. The team stays overweight equities medium-term but has recalibrated to neutral short-term. Tariff uncertainty has eased, providing additional tailwinds. The preference is for quality, durable cash flows, and diversified return sources.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Cumulative global infrastructure spending is projected to reach $106tn through 2040, with transportation, energy, and digital networks the largest funding needs. Hyperscaler AI capex consensus exceeds $660bn in 2026. GSAM identifies a critical allocation gap — Industrials and Materials account for 29.3% and 4.2% of US GVA versus just 9.4% and 2.0% in the MSCI USA Index — making private markets essential to fill the representation gap.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: Defense procurement cycles are accelerating, funnelling demand into dominant defense companies and specialist suppliers. Industrials benefit from supply-chain reconfiguration and reshoring, with multi-year procurement schedules providing earnings visibility. GSAM sees durable structural demand as governments channel capital toward alternative refining capacity and domestic manufacturing.

Source: Market Know-How 1Q 2026
Full Commentary: GSAM argues liquid alternatives earn their place as a decorrelator, particularly when traditional hedges break down. In the base case, flexible bond strategies offer the best fixed-income positioning. In the stagflation scenario, trend-following strategies and income-oriented private assets replace traditional inflation hedges. Multi-strategy hedge funds provide tactical agility across dislocated markets.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: T. Rowe Price maintains a modestly overweight equity stance across both the April update and the UK committee's March positioning. The overweight reflects a balance between still-decent fundamentals — fiscal support, lagged Fed rate cuts, and constructive earnings — and expensive valuations alongside ongoing geopolitical tensions. The firm has reduced the size of the overweight in April and shifted composition away from energy-sensitive regions toward more resilient areas.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees favour UK equities on attractive valuations and a healthy longer-term earnings growth outlook. Budget concerns remain a headwind, and the inflation outlook is volatile due to Middle East energy risks. The Bank of England retains flexibility to cut rates given labour market weakness, which could provide additional support.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Japan remains overweight, supported by improving global growth and fiscal stimulus prospects. Equity strength has been underpinned by these tailwinds, though higher energy costs and potential for tighter monetary policy may temper near-term momentum. The firm views Japan as part of the non-US opportunity set benefiting from broadening market leadership.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: T. Rowe Price maintains growth overweight relative to value. Mega-cap tech faces increased competition and capital intensity, but underlying fundamentals remain strong. The economic growth outlook for 2026 is supported by deregulation, fiscal policy, reshoring, and potential Fed cuts. Valuation has moved ahead of fundamentals, but the structural case for growth-style equities holds.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The small-cap overweight is maintained across both committees. Small-cap earnings are improving sharply from lower rates, deregulation, fiscal stimulus, and broadening AI benefits. An upswing in manufacturing activity is also supportive. Valuations are reasonable relative to large caps, and the supportive earnings outlook is reflected in constructive revisions.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Global high yield remains overweight. Tight spreads may limit further price upside, but the sector offers a healthy yield, measured default expectations, and a low duration profile that provides insulation against rate volatility. The overweight also serves as an inflation-sensitive allocation within the broader fixed income positioning.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The UK committee maintains an overweight to sterling versus the US dollar. The interest rate differential is on par, and the dollar may soften if US economic growth decelerates. However, US growth remains stronger than UK growth, and escalation in the Middle East could trigger flight-to-quality flows to the dollar.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Cash yields remain reasonably attractive, supporting a modest overweight position. However, the firm has moderated the size of the cash overweight, adding some exposure to equities. Cash also provides flexibility to deploy into opportunities that may arise from market volatility and continued geopolitical uncertainty.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Merged view across T. Rowe Price's committees. The March UK committee favoured Europe ex-UK for its fiscal support, improving growth, and cyclical/financial sector tailwinds. The April update pulled back, shifting away from Europe as an energy-sensitive region after the Hormuz disruption pushed oil prices higher and increased LNG supply risks. The net positioning is approximately neutral.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees support an overweight to EM equities. Global growth momentum and stable currencies are supportive, though higher oil prices and energy import exposure are significant near-term risks. The April update reduced EM debt exposure specifically, not EM equities. EM equities benefit from broadening global growth and fiscal support across multiple regions.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees agree on a neutral stance for China. Policy support and improving economic activity aid sentiment, but weak labour markets, ongoing property sector stress, and higher energy costs continue to weigh on domestic demand. The firm does not see a catalyst for upgrading the position in the near term.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The UK committee maintains a neutral stance on inflation-linked bonds. A weak labour market should contribute to easing inflation in the medium term, but the Middle East conflict introduces significant uncertainty about the direction of inflation through its impact on energy prices. The strategic allocation provides an inflation hedge within the broader portfolio.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: UK investment-grade credit is underweight, consistent with T. Rowe Price's broader short duration positioning. Credit fundamentals are still supportive, but spreads are expensive and geopolitical uncertainty could weigh on the asset class. The UK committee rates UK IG as neutral on a standalone basis, but the firm's overall framework — short duration across gilts, US IG, and European IG — implies an underweight to UK IG within the portfolio context. The April update's emphasis on reducing quality bond exposure reinforces the underweight.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The UK committee is overweight EM dollar sovereigns on attractive yields and regional improvements. The April update explicitly reduced EM debt exposure to limit risk amid higher US rates and geopolitical uncertainty. The split reflects a timing difference: the March view is structurally constructive while the April reduction is a tactical risk-management move. On balance, the yield case and regional improvements support an overweight, with the April caution warranting closer monitoring rather than a full downgrade.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees support an overweight to EM local currency debt. Compelling yields combined with expectations for a weaker US dollar and lower rate volatility point to a favourable outlook. The April update reduced EM debt exposure broadly, but the local currency thesis — driven by dollar softening and attractive carry — remains structurally intact across both publications.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees support an overweight to EM corporate debt. The sector offers shorter duration than EM dollar sovereigns, providing insulation against rates volatility. Spreads are tight but total yields remain attractive relative to other fixed income sectors. The shorter duration profile means EM corporates are less exposed to the higher-rates concern that prompted the April reduction in broader EM debt.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The committee is neutral on GBP versus EUR. The interest rate differential favours sterling, and GBP is attractively valued relative to the euro. However, the bar is lower for European data to surprise to the upside, and German fiscal policy is expected to provide euro support.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: The committee remains neutral on GBP versus JPY. The yen's safe-haven currency status provides support during periods of heightened geopolitical uncertainty, which the firm expects to persist in the near term.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: T. Rowe Price maintains a short duration position across its fixed income allocation, reflected in underweights to gilts, US and European investment-grade bonds. The firm has overweights to high yield and cash as alternatives. The April update added back some duration as yields moved higher, but the overall stance remains cautious amid persistent inflation uncertainty.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Both committees agree on an underweight to US equities. Valuations remain stretched as hyperscaler capex pressures sentiment, yet AI infrastructure demand continues to support earnings breadth. Higher oil prices add to inflation uncertainty. The April update shifted toward US large-cap value within the underweight, reflecting the 'heavy metal' rotation away from growth toward more cyclical areas.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: Australia is underweight per the UK committee. Attractive valuations and stronger resource demand provide support, but higher energy prices and restrictive monetary policy may limit upside. The position reflects a view that Australia's exposure to global growth is tempered by domestic policy headwinds.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: UK gilts are underweight as part of the broader short duration positioning. Gilt yields are expected to rise in tandem with government bond yields worldwide. However, the committee notes that a weak labour market may allow the Bank of England to cut rates faster than expected, providing a potential offset.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: US investment-grade aggregate is underweight as part of the short duration stance. Resilient growth amid sticky inflation places upward pressure on US rates. Credit fundamentals remain supportive, but spreads are expensive relative to history, limiting the risk-reward for taking duration exposure through IG bonds.

Source: Monthly Asset Allocation Update – April 2026 | Global Asset Allocation: The View From the UK
Full Commentary: European investment-grade aggregate is underweight. The spike in energy prices is likely to be exacerbated by tight labour markets and German fiscal expansion. The committee expects the ECB to react in a hawkish manner, potentially hiking rates, which would pressure European IG bond prices.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a moderately overweight view on global equities over a 12-month horizon. Strong pre-crisis earnings momentum, intact AI innovation tailwinds, more attractive valuations after the sell-off, and supportive fiscal policy underpin this stance. Historically, in stagflationary periods with oil spikes of at least 20%, markets have posted decent returns within 12 months. The team would need a further downside adjustment or upside catalysts from conflict resolution before taking a stronger overweight view.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington holds a modest overweight on US equities, citing AI dominance and lower sensitivity to energy prices than import-dependent economies. The recent de-rating of mega-cap tech stocks has brought valuations back to attractive levels. Relative earnings fundamentals favour the US over the Eurozone, and investor sentiment surveys are more positive on Europe, suggesting a potential reversal. The team moved to a small overweight on US versus Eurozone equities specifically.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington holds an overweight stance on EM equities, driven by Asia's critical role in the AI supply chain through semiconductors and memory production. AI demand visibility remains strong through 2027. China has demonstrated notable resilience during the conflict thanks to energy stockpiles, diversified supply sources, and available policy levers, with recent activity data surprising to the upside. The team favours EM as a relative trade against the UK.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a modest underweight on Europe ex-UK equities. European outperformance versus the US has increasingly diverged from relative earnings fundamentals, creating vulnerability. Additional headwinds include competition from China, a stronger euro, and elevated energy prices from the Middle East conflict. The balance of risks is skewed against Europe, making it a funding source for preferred positions elsewhere.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a modest underweight on UK equities, driven by a weak earnings outlook relative to other regions. The UK serves as a funding source for positions in areas like EM where the team sees greater relative upside. The UK's relatively high exposure to the energy sector provides some offset to the weak fundamental picture, but not enough to shift the underweight view.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington remains neutral on Japanese equities. The supportive domestic policy backdrop stays intact, with potential fiscal measures to underpin growth. The team acknowledges scope for positive excess returns but prefers to stay neutral pending a more attractive entry opportunity, particularly given the uncertainty surrounding the conflict's duration and spillover effects.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a moderately overweight long-duration stance across global government bonds. Yields spiked as central bank expectations flipped from easing to tightening, with short-term yields jumping 10-60 basis points. The team sees two paths to lower yields: persistently high energy prices driving demand destruction, or de-escalation reducing inflation expectations. Unlike 2022, governments have less fiscal flexibility, rates were already restrictive, and the labour market is not as tight — all factors that should help contain inflation.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington holds a neutral view on US rates, having moved away from a modest underweight earlier in March. Markets repriced expectations to policy on hold following the US-Iran conflict escalation. With no clear directional catalyst, yields are expected to trade in a broad range through 2026, limiting the scope for high-conviction tactical positioning.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a neutral view on European rates after closing a brief intra-month modest overweight on German rates versus the US. The repricing has left eurozone yields at more attractive valuations, and the team believes markets have likely overreacted. However, they prefer to wait for a clearer path toward de-escalation before re-engaging with conviction.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains a neutral stance on UK rates. The Bank of England adopted a more hawkish position following the Middle East escalation, prioritising inflation containment even at the cost of weaker growth. The team is monitoring spillover effects from the conflict and seeking clearer signals on both the policy roadmap and conflict resolution timing.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington maintains an underweight view on Japanese government bonds, the only regional duration underweight in the portfolio. Inflation and fiscal risks are meaningful, yet both the Bank of Japan's reaction function and inflation/real yield repricing have been more muted than in other regions. Energy prices reinforce increasingly structural inflation pressures amid tight labour markets and a weak yen, while fiscal support is turning more proactive.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington upgraded to a modest overweight on European investment-grade credit. Euro credit fundamentals are healthier than in the US, with stronger debt service and leverage metrics. The composition favours asset-heavy sectors rather than software and AI-related leveraged borrowers. Critically, European banks have less exposure to US private credit funds and vehicles, reducing spillover risk from percolating concerns in the private credit space.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington moved to a modest overweight on European high yield after spreads widened from very tight levels. The upgrade represents a measured step back into risk within multi-asset portfolios, with current spread levels providing a degree of cushion against further widening. The European HY position complements the IG overweight as part of a broader European credit preference.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington remains neutral on EM debt, preferring to stay on the sidelines until valuations offer a more compelling entry point. While the team has begun re-engaging selectively through European credit, they maintain a more cautious stance toward EM debt, viewing current spreads as insufficient compensation for the risks.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington shifted oil from underweight to neutral following the Middle East conflict. The hostilities and oil price surge considerably widened the distribution of possible outcomes with a skew toward the higher end. Exceedingly high costs associated with rolling positions at the front end of the curve rendered the underweight risk/reward profile untenable.

Source: QUARTERLY ASSET ALLOCATION OUTLOOK: Q2 2026 | Monthly Asset Allocation Outlook
Full Commentary: Wellington introduced a slight overweight on gold following a 20% decline from its recent peak. Technicals have become more neutral after speculative excess and leverage were largely neutralised. The secular bull case rests on central bank diversification away from the US dollar and expectations for a weaker dollar. The team cautions that gold is not an effective inflation hedge — its primary long-term drivers are negative correlations to the dollar and real yields. The biggest risk is central banks becoming net sellers to fund war-related capital needs.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS favours UK duration as slowing wage growth, fiscal tightening and weakening labour market momentum support a more dovish Bank of England trajectory. Back-loaded tax increases are expected to slow growth, lower inflation and compound labour-market weakness. With policy still above neutral, the BoE retains room to ease further.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Markets have priced in rate hikes on sticky inflation readings, but FTIS believes this is overly optimistic and expects yields to fall if growth forecasts disappoint. The Canadian economy faces risks from US trade restrictions, cyclical sector dependence and a labour market skewed by part-time workers.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: EM equities retain an overweight despite exposure being trimmed on energy vulnerability. Earnings expectations are accelerating rapidly across EMs ex-China, driven by AI capital spending boosting hardware component makers in Asia. Corporate governance improvements and policy support underpin the structural case. Taiwan and South Korea's vulnerability to Strait of Hormuz disruption represents meaningful short-term risk.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: The overweight to Japan has been trimmed from a high-conviction position given vulnerability to energy prices — Japan imports 93% of its crude through the Strait of Hormuz. However, the country holds 200+ days of oil inventory, PM Takaichi's ¥21tn fiscal stimulus targets semiconductors, robotics and defence, and trailing earnings growth of 11.4% with strong revision breadth support the continued overweight.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS has neutralised cross-asset equity positioning as the Middle East conflict continues to drive markets. The macro backdrop has become more challenging — growth indicators weakening, inflation limiting expectations for stimulative monetary policy. However, sentiment and positioning have retreated to attractive levels, supporting risk assets, and corporate fundamentals remain relatively constructive.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS holds bonds at neutral overall, expecting demand destruction to play a greater role in monetary policy decisions than inflation. The recent inflationary uptick in yields may present an opportunity to add selectively to duration amid a weakening macro backdrop. However, rising inflation makes it harder for central banks to stimulate sluggish economies, supporting a defensive approach.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: The recent rise in energy prices means the risks attached to the Middle East conflict now appear adequately priced into markets. Beyond the conflict, increased demand from AI should offer long-term support to energy and industrial metals. Structural diversification benefits from private assets remain attractive.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Elevated short-term yields offer attractive risk-free returns, and FTIS has added slightly to cash amid geopolitical uncertainty. However, the requirement to lower portfolios' interest-rate sensitivity has moderated as yields have risen month-on-month, keeping cash at an overall neutral allocation.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS has added to core US equities as a defensive measure, neutralising style-factor preferences. High-quality, large-cap stocks tend to outperform when the economic outlook falters. Sentiment and positioning have retreated toward contrarian buy signals. However, equities remain overvalued relative to macro volatility. The team's proprietary earnings model expects 13.1% US earnings growth vs consensus 17.6%.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: High-quality growth stocks tend to outperform when the economic outlook falters, and markets have historically rewarded growth names in uncertain conditions. Rotation momentum has been reduced by stagflation risks, which typically impact the real economy. FTIS remains concerned about AI capital investment sustainability, but more immediate macro concerns mean they favour core US equities as a defensive measure.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Corporate fundamentals remain strong, but economic developments have changed the calculus and pushed FTIS to neutralise style-factor preferences. Growth has been slowing, labour data has softened, and core inflation is rising, complicating Fed response. Slowing growth typically weakens cyclical earnings momentum, working against the broadening-out trade that had previously supported value.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Canadian macro indicators were mixed — manufacturing PMI strengthened but services PMI remained weak and trailing earnings declined. The BoC is prioritising inflation, which may require rate hikes to combat energy inflation rather than stimulate the economy. Canada's over-reliance on commodities is a weakness given overextension in materials markets.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: UK consumers have been saving more while disposable income growth has fallen sharply. The UK faces significant macro headwinds including slowing growth, negative employment growth and higher energy prices. However, the UK is a defensive market influenced by global energy majors, and should perform better in volatile conditions. FTIS has improved its view and neutralised positioning.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Macro and corporate fundamentals in China remain weak — property deterioration, stagnant domestic demand, consumer sentiment at all-time lows, and tech earnings eroded by price competition. Stimulus has had limited impact. However, domestic flows from maturing term deposits and foreign inflows during the Middle East conflict provide a modest offset.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS expects demand destruction to outweigh inflation in shaping ECB policy, and Europe is less reliant on Middle East energy than Asia. However, German fiscal expansion and increased sovereign issuance create upward yield pressure, offsetting the dovish impulse.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Recent interest-rate increases have moved JGB yields closer to equilibrium. However, the real policy rate remains below zero, meaning the BoJ remains stimulative. This gives the BoJ room to raise rates further to combat energy-related inflation and support the yen, particularly against a background of additional fiscal stimulus.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: The RBA raised rates in March amid firmer inflation and tight labour market conditions. The RBA remains data-dependent, viewing tight labour, sticky inflation and a stable macro environment as reasons for restrictive policy. However, relatively weak economic growth and negative trailing earnings may change that calculus.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: IG credit benefits from resilient corporate fundamentals and improved guidance, but spreads remain relatively narrow, making the risk/return profile less attractive in a risk-off environment. FTIS prefers government bond defensiveness and holds IG at neutral.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: HY spreads have widened but remain tight and do not fully reflect risks from tightening financial conditions. Spreads have not widened in line with the equity sell-off — investors are not getting the usual cushion of falling rates. The balance of risk and reward favours neutrality.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: FTIS has trimmed exposure to EM local-currency debt amid a strengthening US dollar and growing risks to macro stability in Asia. The long-term structural case remains intact but near-term risks have prompted a neutralisation.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Markets no longer price in Fed rate cuts despite slowing growth signals, reflecting sticky core inflation and energy price concerns. Rising fiscal deficits and tax cuts add upward yield pressure. The FOMC revised core PCE projections to 2.7% for end-2026. FTIS stays underweight US duration.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: The weakening macro backdrop is negative for small caps, compounded by hawkish monetary policy expectations. An energy-cost shock disproportionately impacts small caps given lower pricing power and higher operating leverage. The Russell 2000's tilt toward financials, industrials and consumer discretionary exposes it to energy volatility.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: Manufacturing activity continues to soften due to structural weaknesses in Germany. A subdued macro backdrop, higher energy prices and weak domestic demand could weigh on eurozone equities, reflected in thinner profit margins and narrower leadership. However, FTIS has trimmed the underweight as these markets have less direct exposure to Middle East energy than Asian counterparts.

Source: Allocation Views: From Goldilocks to geopolitics—repositioning for an energy shock
Full Commentary: The RBA raised rates in both February and March on hotter-than-expected inflation, and growth has begun to slow. Weak demand from China limits industrial production momentum. Corporate earnings revisions are weak relative to other regions, trailing earnings are negative, and equity market breadth has contracted.
